questions: At CyanGate, you led the development of the **JSON-to-JSON AI Agentic System**, which required integrating multiple LLM providers (OpenAI, Copilot, Gemini, Anthropic) and building a Python validation engine to cut costs and latency by 50%. During this project, you likely encountered moments where the path forward was unclear—perhaps conflicting API behaviors, unexpected validation failures, or ambiguous client requirements. **Walk me through a specific instance where you had to make a high-stakes decision without clear guidance.** How did you balance speed with risk? What tradeoffs did you consider, and how did you rally your team or stakeholders around your approach?

Zephlen’s culture emphasizes *‘Radical Candor & Low Ego’*—we debate ideas vigorously but commit once decisions are made. In your **Atlassian Jira Intelligence System** project, you worked with cross-functional teams (including non-technical stakeholders) to replace manual ticket searches with an AI-powered assistant. **Describe a time when you had to push back against a stakeholder’s request or challenge a teammate’s technical approach.** How did you frame your feedback to focus on the problem, not the person? Did the outcome change the project’s direction, and how did the team react to your candor?

The **Automation Workflow Generation System** you built at CyanGate involved a multi-agent architecture with specialized agents for flow execution, security, and validation—all while integrating bash access and web search capabilities. This kind of complexity often reveals edge cases no one anticipated. **Tell me about a time when one of your AI systems failed spectacularly in production (e.g., an agent looped infinitely, hallucinated a critical API call, or exposed sensitive data).** How did you diagnose the root cause under pressure? What safeguards or cultural practices (e.g., blameless postmortems, automated testing) did you put in place to prevent recurrence, and how did you communicate the incident to leadership or clients?
